steps.title: Simplify the first equation to standard form
steps.content:
To work with the system, rewrite the first equation with all terms on one side:
$$x + 33y - 200 = -359$$
Add 200 to both sides:
$$x + 33y = -159$$
Now both equations are in the form $ax + by = c$.

steps.title: Recognize the condition for infinitely many solutions
steps.content: A system of two linear equations in two variables has infinitely many solutions when the two equations represent the same line. This happens if and only if one equation is a scalar multiple of the other—that is, the coefficients and constant terms are proportional.

steps.title: Set up the proportionality condition and solve for $w$
steps.content:
For the equations to represent the same line, the ratio of corresponding coefficients must be equal:
$$\frac{\text{coefficient of } x \text{ in eq. 2}}{\text{coefficient of } x \text{ in eq. 1}} = \frac{\text{coefficient of } y \text{ in eq. 2}}{\text{coefficient of } y \text{ in eq. 1}} = \frac{\text{constant in eq. 2}}{\text{constant in eq. 1}}$$
$$\frac{2w}{1} = \frac{11}{33} = \frac{-53}{-159}$$
Simplify the right-hand ratios:
$$\frac{11}{33} = \frac{1}{3} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{-53}{-159} = \frac{53}{159} = \frac{1}{3} \checkmark$$
Since both equal $\frac{1}{3}$, set the first ratio equal to this value:
$$\frac{2w}{1} = \frac{1}{3}$$
$$2w = \frac{1}{3}$$
$$w = \frac{1}{6}$$
Multiply the second equation by 3 to verify: $3(2wx + 11y) = 3(-53)$ gives $6wx + 33y = -159$, which when $w = \frac{1}{6}$ becomes $x + 33y = -159$ — exactly the first equation. That matches the requirement for infinitely many solutions — $w = \frac{1}{6}$ (or approximately 0.167) is correct.
